query: Supracondylar fracture of humerus vs lateral condylar fracture

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Lateral Humeral Condyle Fractures -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The following conditions must be ruled out when evaluating pediatric lateral condylar fractures: Other ipsilateral upper limb fractures Supracondylar fractures Ipsilateral posterolateral or posteromedial elbow dislocation These conditions require different management approaches and may be distinguished from pediatric lateral condylar fractures after a thorough evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: Supracondylar Humerus Fractures -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Children and their parents/carers should be counseled about the severity and prognosis of these injuries. Nondisplaced and minimally displaced fractures have the best long-term outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Lateral Humeral Condyle Fractures -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Lateral humeral condyle fractures are the second most commonly encountered elbow fractures after the supracondylar type in the pediatric age group. These injuries are peculiar as they are intraarticular but displacement-prone owing to the forearm extensor muscles' attachment on the lateral epicondyle close to the condylar area. Thus, complications, including elbow deformity, can emerge if this condition is not appropriately treated.

retrieved_docs.title: Supracondylar Humerus Fractures -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: All patients should be assessed according to Advanced Trauma Life Support (ATLS) principles. A non-accidental injury should be excluded from all pediatric injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Elbow Dislocation -- Differential Diagnosis -- Fractures:
retrieved_docs.content: Radial head or neck fracture Olecranon fracture Distal humerus fracture (supracondylar or intercondylar) Coronoid process fracture

retrieved_docs.title: Supracondylar Humerus Fractures -- Prognosis -- Injury-Specific Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Grade of injury Adequacy of reduction and fixation Neurovascular status of the limb

retrieved_docs.title: Supracondylar Humerus Fractures -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: A significant proportion of the distal humerus is cartilaginous in children. Although the ossification centers appear predictably around the elbow, there may be diagnostic uncertainty in very young children. Differential diagnoses include Radial head fractures Distal humeral medial or lateral condyle fractures Transphyseal fractures of the distal humerus are another differential diagnosis, and non-accidental injury should be excluded in such cases. A pulled elbow is usually indicated from the history, and radiographs are rarely performed in these cases.

retrieved_docs.title: Supracondylar Humerus Fractures -- Pearls and Other Issues -- Pitfalls
retrieved_docs.content: Pin placement at the fracture site needs to be maximized. Lateral pins that cross at the fracture site or crossed pins that cross in the distal fragment have little to no rotational stability.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Lateral Humeral Condyle Fractures -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: However, pseudo-cubitus varus has been reported to be more prevalent than true cubitus varus. A 3.7 mm increase in interepicondylar width can predict true cubitus varus. The risk of cubitus varus increases in Song classification stages 3, 4, and 5. [43]

retrieved_docs.title: Supracondylar Humerus Fractures -- Complications -- 5. Stiffness
retrieved_docs.content: The range of movement rapidly improves within the first six weeks following surgery. The range can improve to 98% of normal within the first 12 months. [57] Stiffness is more commonly associated with open reduction procedures. [46] Long-term reduction in the range of movement is also associated with malunion.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Lateral Humeral Condyle Fractures -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Lateral humeral condyle fractures are the second most commonly encountered pediatric elbow fractures next to supracondylar fractures (see Image . Lateral Humeral Condyle Fracture). These injuries are unique, being intraarticular but vulnerable to displacement and nonunion owing to the forearm extensors' pull on the lateral epicondyle near the condylar area. These fractures can result in complications like elbow deformity if not appropriately managed. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Supracondylar Humerus Fractures -- Treatment / Management -- Percutaneous Pinning Technique
retrieved_docs.content: Lateral pins and crossed pins (medial and lateral) bicortical constructs can be used.

retrieved_docs.title: Supracondylar Humerus Fractures -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Appropriate treatment on time improves the long-term prognosis of these injuries. The prognosis may be worse for higher-grade injuries; however, anatomical reduction, stable fixation, and appropriate care of the soft tissues (including neurovascular injuries and compartment syndrome) improve outcomes. Nondisplaced Gartland I fractures usually heal well without complication. Gartland II fractures with medial comminution can develop cubitus varus if this is not recognized, reduced, and stabilized. Gartland III and IV injuries are more likely to be associated with neurovascular injuries; however, timely, appropriate treatment can lead to excellent outcomes. [54]

retrieved_docs.title: Supracondylar Humerus Fractures -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis depends on the remodeling potential of the bone, patient, and injury-specific factors.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Lateral Humeral Condyle Fractures -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Supracondylar fractures may also present with the same history and physical findings. However, this condition is more commonly associated with more severe elbow deformity and neurovascular deficits than lateral condyle injuries. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1195
retrieved_docs.content: Fracture of the shoulder and upper arm (Fractuur van schouder en bovenarm) and fracture of the upper limb (fracture of upper limb) are both classified as subtypes of a clavicle fracture (كسر الترقوة). The clavicle fracture itself is regarded as a specific form of a more general limb fracture (كسر في الأطراف). Clinical observations indicate that clavicle fractures (كسر الترقوة) frequently co‑occur with femur bone fractures (كسر عظم الفخذ). This hierarchical relationship highlights that injuries to the shoulder, upper arm, and broader upper‑limb region are encompassed within the category of clavicular injuries, which are part of the overall spectrum of limb fractures, and that patients presenting with a clavicle fracture should be evaluated for possible concurrent femoral fractures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 789
retrieved_docs.content: Fractuur van schouder en bovenarm (fracture of the shoulder and upper arm) and the broader category fracture of upper limb are both classified as subtypes of كسر الترقوة (clavicle fracture). In addition, كسر الترقوة frequently co‑occurs with كسر عظم الفخذ (femur fracture), indicating that injuries to the clavicle are often associated with fractures of the thigh bone. This hierarchical relationship and the noted co‑occurrence underscore the importance of evaluating patients with clavicle fractures for possible additional upper‑limb or lower‑limb injuries to ensure comprehensive trauma care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 877
retrieved_docs.content: The late effect of fracture of multiple bones, the late consequences of fracture of the skull and facial bones, and the late effects of fracture of the femoral neck are all described as being associated with a fracture (كسر). In addition, the open reduction of an open shoulder dislocation with fracture of the greater tuberosity is identified as a direct morphological manifestation of a fracture (كسر). Together, these statements highlight that both delayed sequelae across various skeletal regions and specific surgical interventions are directly linked to the underlying presence of a fracture, emphasizing the need for comprehensive assessment and management of fracture‑related pathology in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 161
retrieved_docs.content: reduction of closed greater tuberosity fracture** and **open reduction of closed humeral fracture with external skeletal fixation** are classified by the morphological descriptor **“كسر مغلق” (closed fracture)**. Additional closed‑fracture operations—open reduction of closed humeral fracture with internal skeletal fixation, closed radial shaft fracture, closed scapular fracture, closed ulnar shaft fracture, and closed clavicular fracture—are also child procedures of the same shoulder‑and‑arm repair category. A broader procedural concept, **internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction**, serves as a parent node for treatment groups across multiple anatomical regions (hip and femur, other lower‑extremity bones, radius and ulna). **Internal fixation of clavicle without fracture reduction** is a specific instance of this paradigm, connecting clavicular management to the same non‑reduction fixation strategy used for other long‑bone injuries. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 237
retrieved_docs.content: head injury trauma also show strong associations with falls, and head injury trauma often co‑occurs with shoulder fracture (كسر الكتف). Fracture hierarchies place clavicle fracture (كسر الترقوة) as a subclass of limb fractures (كسر في الأطراف). Clavicle fracture subsumes fractures of the shoulder and upper arm (Fractuur van schouder en bovenarm) and generic upper‑limb fractures (fracture of upper limb), and it co‑occurs with shoulder fracture, femur fracture (كسر عظم الفخذ) and lower‑limb fracture (كسر الطرف السفلي). Lower‑limb fracture frequently co‑occurs with upper‑limb fracture. In the upper extremity, wrist bone fracture (عظم رسغ مكسور) functions as a parent of upper‑limb fracture and, via an inverse_isa relationship, is also defined as hand fracture (كسر اليد). Hand fracture belongs to both “fractures of limbs” and “fractures and dislocations of limbs”. Wrist injury is therefore linked to a closed, unspecified distal forearm fracture, connecting wrist injuries with broader

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 713
retrieved_docs.content: The open reduction of closed greater tuberosity fracture, open reduction of closed humeral fracture with external skeletal fixation, open reduction of closed humeral fracture with internal skeletal fixation, open reduction of closed radial shaft fracture, open reduction of closed scapular fracture, juxta‑articular, and open reduction of closed ulnar shaft fracture are all surgical interventions that share the direct morphology of a closed fracture (كسر مغلق). Each procedure targets a specific site of an upper‑limb fracture, and the broader category of fracture of the upper limb is associated with this closed‑fracture morphology. Recognizing that these diverse open‑reduction techniques—whether employing external or internal skeletal fixation—are unified by the underlying closed fracture condition highlights the importance of precise fracture classification and appropriate surgical planning in orthopedic patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 750
retrieved_docs.content: Fracture (كسر) serves as the central concept linking a range of clinical entities. The conditions “Late effect of fracture of multiple bones,” “Late gevolgen van fractuur van schedel en aangezichtsbeenderen” (late consequences of skull and facial bone fracture), “الآثار المتأخرة لكسر الأطراف العلوية” (late effects of upper‑limb fracture), and “الآثار المتأخرة لكسر عنق عظم الفخذ” (late effects of femoral‑neck fracture) are all associated with fracture, indicating that delayed complications can arise in various anatomical regions after a bone break. Additionally, the procedure “Open reduction of open shoulder dislocation with fracture of greater tuberosity” is described as a direct morphological manifestation of fracture, reflecting the specific surgical management required when a fracture involves the greater tuberosity of the humerus in the context of an open shoulder dislocation. Together, these relationships emphasize that fractures can lead to diverse late sequelae across the skeletal system and may necessitate targeted operative interventions that directly address the underlying bony injury.

This morphology is emphasized across diverse procedures to distinguish closed fractures in orthopedic planning and documentation. Closed fractures (“كسر مغلق”) and the condition of osteoporosis or low bone density (هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام (SMQ)) are linked as related concepts, indicating that closed fractures are manifestations of reduced bone strength. The specific injury “fracture of neck of femur (hip)” is classified within the osteoporosis/low bone density SMQ, reinforcing the association between hip fractures and compromised bone quality. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 812
retrieved_docs.content: Fractuur van schouder en bovenarm and fracture of upper limb are both classified under كسر الترقوة, indicating that the clavicle fracture serves as a broader parent category for these upper‑limb injury terms. In clinical practice, كسر الترقوة frequently co‑occurs with كسر الكتف (shoulder fracture) and كسر عظم الفخذ (femur fracture), reflecting a common pattern of multiple skeletal injuries in patients who sustain trauma to the clavicle. Recognizing that clavicle fractures are linked to both other upper‑limb fracture descriptors and to concurrent shoulder and femur fractures helps clinicians anticipate associated injuries, prioritize comprehensive imaging, and coordinate multidisciplinary management to improve patient outcomes.

By connecting specific fracture entities, a comprehensive set of internal‑fixation and open‑reduction techniques, and hierarchical procedural categories to underlying bone‑density disorders, the knowledge base supports integrated treatment planning for orthopedic injuries of the hip, lower extremity, and shoulder‑arm region, while highlighting the importance of managing osteoporosis to reduce fracture risk and optimize surgical outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 1018
retrieved_docs.content: Fractuur van schouder en bovenarm is classified as a subtype of كسر الترقوة, indicating that injuries to the shoulder and upper arm are considered within the broader category of clavicle fractures. Clinical observations show that كسر الترقوة frequently co‑occurs with كسر الكتف, reflecting a common pattern of simultaneous damage to the clavicle and the shoulder region. In addition, كسر الترقوة also co‑occurs with كسر عظم الفخذ, suggesting that patients with clavicle fractures may sustain concurrent femoral bone injuries. Recognizing these associations is essential for comprehensive evaluation and coordinated management of patients presenting with clavicle‑related trauma, as it prompts clinicians to assess for additional shoulder or femur fractures that may impact treatment planning and outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 1037
retrieved_docs.content: Open reduction of closed greater tuberosity fracture, Open reduction of closed humeral fracture with external skeletal fixation, Open reduction of closed humeral fracture with internal skeletal fixation, Open reduction of closed scapular fracture, juxta‑articular, and Open reduction of closed ulnar shaft fracture are surgical interventions that are specifically classified as having the direct morphology “كسر مغلق” (closed fracture). Each of these procedures addresses a closed fracture of an upper‑limb bone, and the term “fracture of upper limb” is also associated with the same morphology “كسر مغلق”. This indicates that closed fractures of the humerus, scapula, greater tuberosity, and ulna are considered part of the broader category of upper‑limb fractures, and that open reduction—whether performed with external or internal skeletal fixation—is the operative approach used to manage these closed injuries. Understanding that these diverse fracture types share the common morphology “كسر مغلق” helps clinicians coordinate diagnosis, treatment planning, and postoperative care for patients with upper‑limb skeletal trauma.

These interventions address fractures, dislocations, non‑unions and malunions of the shoulder girdle and adjacent long bones. Closed fractures (كسر مغلق) of the femoral neck (fracture of neck of femur, hip) and of other long bones are classified under the osteoporosis/low bone density SMQ (هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام). The closed‑fracture morphology is a common descriptor for the listed open‑reduction procedures, reflecting that such injuries frequently occur in patients with reduced bone quality. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 562
retrieved_docs.content: Open reduction of closed greater tuberosity fracture, Open reduction of closed humeral fracture with external skeletal fixation, Open reduction of closed humeral fracture with internal skeletal fixation, Open reduction of closed radial shaft fracture, Open reduction of closed scapular fracture, juxta‑articular, and Open reduction of closed ulnar shaft fracture are all orthopedic procedures whose direct morphology is described as كسر مغلق (a closed fracture). The condition fracture of lower limb is associated with كسر مغلق, indicating that lower‑limb injuries are commonly classified as closed fractures. Moreover, fracture of lower limb is defined as كسر جزء غير محدد من عنق عظم الفخذ، مغلق, meaning it is a closed fracture of an unspecified portion of the femoral neck. Together, these relationships illustrate that a variety of bone‑specific closed fractures—whether of the greater tuberosity, humerus, radial shaft, scapula, ulna, or femoral neck—share the same fundamental morphological classification and are linked in clinical reasoning and surgical management.
